commands.review_summary:
Adds an official external `@openclaw/mxc-sandbox` plugin that registers a Windows MXC ProcessContainer sandbox backend with docs, tests, labeler mapping, package metadata, and dependency/shrinkwrap updates.

PR surface: Source +2187, Tests +1976, Docs +211, Config +67, Other +25. Total +4466 across 32 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a new feature PR; there is no high-confidence real Windows packaged-run proof for the claimed backend behavior yet.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] This needs maintainer/security review and contributor-provided Windows proof rather than an autonomous repair job.

target.title: feat(mxc): add Windows MXC sandbox backend
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw does not currently have an official installable MXC sandbox backend for Windows hosts that can run sandboxed commands through Microsoft eXecution Containers. This leaves MXC-capable Windows environments without a first-party plugin path for ProcessContainer-backed execution and filesystem bridge operations.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an official OpenShell-style external `mxc` sandbox plugin package (`@openclaw/mxc-sandbox`) with Windows-only readiness checks, plugin-owned MXC SDK dependency ownership, ProcessContainer payload construction, a plugin-side launcher, host-backed filesystem bridge support, sandbox policy loading, and targeted tests.

The plugin source lives under `extensions/mxc`, but it is packaged as an installable plugin (`bundledDist: false`) so the MXC SDK/native dependency is owned by the plugin package instead of an unshipped bundled `node_modules` tree. The backend uses the stable current MXC `0.7.0-alpha` schema and keeps sandbox defaults conservative: curated environments, headless UI, per-exec temp directories, absolute binary overrides, host-derived Windows roots, and fail-closed unsupported policy handling.

## User Impact

Users on MXC-capable Windows hosts can install the official MXC sandbox plugin, restart the Gateway, configure `sandbox.backend: "mxc"`, and run OpenClaw sandboxed commands through MXC. Non-Windows or unsupported Windows hosts remain unaffected because the plugin stays dormant when readiness probes fail.

Install command:

```bash
openclaw plugins install @openclaw/mxc-sandbox
```

## Evidence

- `pnpm test:extension mxc -- --reporter=verbose` — passed, 10 files / 88 tests.
- `pnpm test extensions\mxc -- --reporter=verbose` — passed, 10 files / 88 tests.
- `pnpm test:changed` — passed, MXC extension shard.
- `pnpm release:plugins:npm:check -- --plugins @openclaw/mxc-sandbox` — passed; publishable plugin metadata looks OK.
- `pnpm release:plugins:clawhub:check -- --plugins @openclaw/mxc-sandbox` — passed; publishable plugin metadata looks OK.
- `pnpm check` — passed, including import cycle check with 0 runtime value cycles.
- `pnpm build` — passed.
- `pnpm deps:changes:report -- --base-ref origin/main --markdown <session>/dependency-changes.md --json <session>/dependency-changes.json` — generated dependency report: 5 dependency files changed; added `@microsoft/mxc-sdk@0.7.0` and `node-pty@1.2.0-beta.13`; changed resolved package entries for `@clack/core`, `@clack/prompts`, `node-addon-api`, and `undici`.
- `python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --engine copilot --mode branch --base origin/main` — clean after retry; no accepted/actionable findings; overall patch is correct (0.7).

Full `pnpm test` was attempted per CONTRIBUTING, but the broad local suite hit unrelated existing non-MXC failures in UI/TUI/agent runtime tests before completion. The changed/MXC-specific lanes above passed cleanly.

Real Windows MXC behavior proof is still blocked in this environment because the local Crabbox wrapper cannot find a usable Crabbox binary (`crabbox` missing and `..\crabbox\bin\crabbox` absent). I can add real package/install MXC proof once an MXC-capable Windows Crabbox/Testbox path or local MXC host is available.

## Notes

- Contributor changelog edit was removed per CONTRIBUTING.
- `.github/labeler.yml` maps `extensions/mxc/**` to `plugin: mxc`.
- Upstream label verification found no existing `plugin: mxc` label; label creation requires maintainer permissions.
- Dependency Graph Guard still requires maintainer/security `/allow-dependencies-change` approval for the current head SHA after this push.
